ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese as the dynasty's own language. ETHICS-002: Shang boundaries are highly approximate; oracle bone inscriptions provide limited geographic evidence. Archaeological evidence from Anyang and Zhengzhou forms primary basis for territorial estimates.

name_variants.name: Yin Dynasty
name_variants.context: Late Shang period name from capital at Yin

name_variants.name: 殷商
name_variants.context: Alternative Chinese name emphasizing late capital

territory_changes.description: Shang overthrew the Xia dynasty, establishing control over the central Yellow River plain
Capital moved to Yin (Anyang), consolidating Shang control over northern China

sources.citation: Keightley, D.N. (1999). 'The Shang: China's First Historical Dynasty.' Cambridge History of Ancient China.
Sima Qian. Shiji (史記), 'Yin Benji' chapter. c. 94 BC.
Chang, K.C. (1980). Shang Civilization. Yale University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Zhou boundaries represent the Western Zhou at peak extent; the Eastern Zhou (770-256 BC) saw fragmentation into warring states, making a single polygon misleading for the later period. This boundary represents c. 900 BC.

name_variants.name: Eastern Zhou
name_variants.context: Period after capital moved to Luoyang

territory_changes.description: Zhou forces defeated Shang at the Battle of Muye, overthrowing the Shang dynasty
Quanrong nomads sacked the Western Zhou capital, forcing relocation to Luoyang and beginning the decline of royal authority

sources.citation: Shaughnessy, E.L. (1999). 'Western Zhou History.' Cambridge History of Ancient China.
Sima Qian. Shiji (史記), 'Zhou Benji' chapter. c. 94 BC.
Li, Feng (2006). Landscape and Power in Early China: The Crisis and Fall of the Western Zhou. Cambridge University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Qin unification involved brutal military conquest of six rival states and forced mass relocations. The burning of books and burying of scholars (焚書坑儒) was a systematic attempt to erase competing cultural traditions. Forced labor on the Great Wall and Terracotta Army caused massive civilian casualties.

name_variants.name: Qin Dynasty
name_variants.context: English academic convention; origin of the name 'China'

territory_changes.description: Qin Shi Huang completed the conquest of all six rival Warring States, unifying China for the first time under centralized imperial rule
Qin armies conquered the Baiyue peoples of southern China, extending imperial control to modern Guangdong, Guangxi and northern Vietnam

sources.citation: Bodde, D. (1986). 'The State and Empire of Ch'in.' Cambridge History of China, vol. 1.
Sima Qian. Shiji (史記), 'Qin Shi Huang Benji' chapter. c. 94 BC.
Lewis, M.E. (2007). The Early Chinese Empires: Qin and Han. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Han expansion into Central Asia, Korea, and Vietnam involved military conquest and forced assimilation of indigenous populations. The term 'Han Chinese' (漢族) derives from this dynasty and its use as an ethnic label carries implications for minority identity politics in modern China.

name_variants.name: Eastern Han / 東漢
name_variants.context: Post-restoration period with capital at Luoyang

territory_changes.description: Han Emperor Wu conquered the Nanyue kingdom, incorporating modern Guangdong, Guangxi, and northern Vietnam into the empire
Han forces conquered Gojoseon (ancient Korea), establishing four commanderies including Lelang near modern Pyongyang

sources.citation: Ban Gu (班固). Hanshu (漢書). c. 80 AD.
Twitchett, D. & Loewe, M. (1986). Cambridge History of China, vol. 1: The Ch'in and Han Empires.
Lewis, M.E. (2007). The Early Chinese Empires: Qin and Han. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Tang expansion into Central Asia, Tibet, and Korea involved extensive military campaigns. The An Lushan Rebellion (755-763) caused an estimated 13-36 million deaths, one of the deadliest conflicts in human history. Tang cosmopolitanism included both cultural exchange and imperial domination of tributary states.

territory_changes.description: Tang Emperor Taizong defeated and absorbed the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, extending Chinese control over Mongolia and Central Asia
Tang-Silla alliance conquered Goguryeo and Baekje, temporarily extending Tang control over much of the Korean peninsula before Silla pushed Tang forces north

sources.citation: Liu Xu (劉昫). Jiu Tangshu (舊唐書). 945 AD.
Twitchett, D. (1979). Cambridge History of China, vol. 3: Sui and T'ang China.
Lewis, M.E. (2009). China's Cosmopolitan Empire: The Tang Dynasty. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Northern Song boundary represents pre-1127 extent; after the Jurchen Jin conquest of northern China, the Southern Song was confined south of the Huai River. The fall of Kaifeng (1127) involved mass enslavement of the imperial family and civilians (Jingkang Incident).

name_variants.name: Northern Song / 北宋
name_variants.context: Period before Jurchen conquest of the north

territory_changes.description: Zhao Kuangyin usurped power from the Later Zhou, gradually reunifying most of China proper through a mix of diplomacy and military action
Jurchen Jin forces captured the Song capital Kaifeng, enslaving the emperor and 3,000 members of the imperial court (Jingkang Incident), forcing Song retreat south

sources.citation: Tuotuo (脫脫). Songshi (宋史). 1345.
Kuhn, D. (2009). The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese as the dynasty adopted Chinese naming conventions, though the ruling elite were Mongol. ETHICS-002: Yuan dynasty was established by Kublai Khan as the Mongol successor state ruling China. The Mongol conquest of Song China caused massive civilian casualties, including the massacre at Changzhou and other cities. The Four-Class System (四等人制) institutionalized ethnic hierarchy placing Mongols at the top and southern Chinese at the bottom.

name_variants.name: Dai Ön Yeke Mongghul Ulus
name_variants.context: Mongol name meaning 'Great Yuan Great Mongol State'

territory_changes.description: Mongol forces captured the Southern Song capital Lin'an (Hangzhou), ending Song resistance in most of China. Systematic massacres occurred at several resisting cities.
First Mongol invasion of Japan failed due to typhoon ('kamikaze') and Japanese resistance at Hakata Bay

sources.citation: Song Lian (宋濂). Yuanshi (元史). 1370.
Rossabi, M. (1988). Khubilai Khan: His Life and Times. University of California Press.
Rashid al-Din Hamadani. Jami al-Tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles). c. 1307.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese. ETHICS-002: Ming dynasty expelled the Mongol Yuan rulers and restored Han Chinese rule, but also conducted military campaigns against Mongols, Vietnamese, and indigenous peoples of the southwest. The Yongle Emperor's naval expeditions under Zheng He projected Chinese power across Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean.

territory_changes.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, a peasant rebel leader, overthrew the Mongol Yuan dynasty and established the Ming, restoring Han Chinese rule
Ming forces invaded and annexed Vietnam (Đại Ngu), beginning a 20-year occupation resisted by Vietnamese independence movements

sources.citation: Zhang Tingyu (張廷玉). Mingshi (明史). 1739.
Mote, F.W. & Twitchett, D. (1988). Cambridge History of China, vol. 7: The Ming Dynasty.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Sanskrit, the court and literary language of the Gupta period. ETHICS-002: Often called India's 'Golden Age' in historiography, but this narrative sometimes obscures the continuation of caste hierarchy and patriarchal structures during this period. Territorial expansion under Samudragupta involved the subjugation of numerous regional kingdoms.

name_variants.name: Gupta Empire
name_variants.source: Majumdar, History and Culture of the Indian People

territory_changes.description: Samudragupta's Digvijaya campaigns conquered or subjugated dozens of kingdoms across north and central India, as documented in the Allahabad Pillar Inscription
Huna (Hephthalite) invasions began eroding Gupta control over western territories, contributing to imperial decline

sources.citation: Allahabad Pillar Inscription (Prayaga-prashasti) of Samudragupta. c. 350 AD.
Majumdar, R.C. (1954). The Classical Age (History and Culture of the Indian People, vol. 3). Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan.
Faxian (法顯). Record of Buddhistic Kingdoms (佛國記). c. 414 AD.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Tamil (சோழ நாடு), the language of the Chola homeland. ETHICS-002: The Chola Empire was a major maritime power that projected military force across Southeast Asia, including the invasion of Srivijaya (1025). Chola conquests in Sri Lanka involved destruction of Buddhist monasteries and the Anuradhapura kingdom. The long temporal span encompasses multiple distinct political phases.

name_variants.name: Medieval Chola Empire
name_variants.context: Period of imperial expansion under Vijayalaya and successors

territory_changes.description: Rajendra Chola I launched a massive naval expedition against the Srivijaya Empire, raiding ports across Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula to secure trade routes
Rajaraja Chola I conquered the Anuradhapura kingdom of Sri Lanka, destroying Buddhist monasteries and relocating the Sinhalese capital

sources.citation: Nilakanta Sastri, K.A. (1955). The Cōḷas. University of Madras.
Tiruvalangadu copper plates of Rajendra Chola I. c. 1025 AD.
Kulke, H. & Rothermund, D. (2004). A History of India. Routledge.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Marathi, the language of the Maratha homeland. ETHICS-002: The Maratha Empire arose as a Hindu resistance movement against Mughal rule. While often celebrated as a liberation movement, Maratha expansion involved devastating raids (particularly in Bengal and the Carnatic) that caused significant civilian suffering. The Peshwa-era expansion was extractive toward conquered regions.

name_variants.name: Maratha Confederacy
name_variants.context: Peshwa period when power was distributed among confederate chiefs
name_variants.source: Sen, S.N. Military System of the Marathas

territory_changes.description: Shivaji Maharaj crowned himself Chhatrapati, formally establishing Maratha sovereignty independent from Mughal and Bijapur rule
Third Anglo-Maratha War: British East India Company defeated the Maratha Confederacy, annexing its territories and ending the last major Indian power opposing British colonialism

sources.citation: Gordon, S. (1993). The Marathas 1600-1818. Cambridge University Press (New Cambridge History of India).
Sabhasad Bakhar (सभासद बखर). Late 17th century Marathi chronicle.
Sen, S.N. (1958). Military System of the Marathas. Orient Longmans.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Urdu/Persian, the court language. ETHICS-002: The Delhi Sultanate's establishment and expansion involved extensive destruction of Hindu and Buddhist temples and forced conversions, particularly under Alauddin Khalji and Muhammad bin Tughlaq. Timur's sack of Delhi (1398) resulted in massive civilian massacres. Multiple successive dynasties (Mamluk, Khalji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, Lodi) are grouped under this single entity.

name_variants.name: سلطنت دهلی
name_variants.context: Persian form used in court documents

name_variants.name: दिल्ली सल्तनत
name_variants.source: Habib, I. Atlas of the Mughal Empire

territory_changes.description: Qutb ud-Din Aibak established the Mamluk dynasty in Delhi after the Ghurid conquests, creating the first major Islamic state in India
Timur invaded and sacked Delhi, massacring an estimated 100,000 prisoners before the battle and devastating the city, fatally weakening the Tughlaq Sultanate

sources.citation: Jackson, P. (2003). The Delhi Sultanate: A Political and Military History. Cambridge University Press.
Barani, Ziauddin. Tarikh-i Firuz Shahi. c. 1357.
Eaton, R.M. (2000). 'Temple Desecration and Indo-Muslim States.' Journal of Islamic Studies.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Kannada, the primary language of the capital region. Telugu, Tamil, and Sanskrit were also used. ETHICS-002: Vijayanagara positioned itself as a defender of Hindu dharma against the Deccan Sultanates, but this religious framing can obscure the complex political alliances that crossed religious lines. The Battle of Talikota (1565) resulted in the complete destruction of the capital and mass slaughter.

name_variants.name: విజయనగర సామ్రాజ్యము
name_variants.context: Telugu form; Telugu was widely used in the empire's later periods

territory_changes.description: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I established Vijayanagara as a Hindu kingdom resisting the southern expansion of the Delhi Sultanate
Allied Deccan Sultanates defeated Vijayanagara at the Battle of Talikota (Rakshasi-Tangadi). The capital was sacked and destroyed over months, with systematic demolition of temples and palaces

sources.citation: Stein, B. (1989). Vijayanagara (New Cambridge History of India). Cambridge University Press.
Sewell, R. (1900). A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar.
Krishnadevaraya. Amuktamalyada. c. 1520.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Sanskrit; the Kushans used the Bactrian language (written in Greek script) as their administrative language, but Sanskrit was dominant in their Indian territories. The Kushan self-designation in Bactrian was ΚΟϷΑΝΟ (Koshano). ETHICS-002: The Kushan Empire was a conduit for cultural exchange along the Silk Road, facilitating the spread of Buddhism from India to Central and East Asia. Their multi-ethnic and multi-religious character resists simplistic categorization.

name_variants.name: Kushan Empire
name_variants.source: Rosenfield, The Dynastic Arts of the Kushans

name_variants.name: ΚΟϷΑΝΟ
name_variants.context: Bactrian language self-designation on coins and inscriptions

territory_changes.description: Under Kanishka I, the Kushan Empire reached its greatest extent, spanning from the Aral Sea to the Ganges plain, controlling key Silk Road trade routes
Sasanian Empire under Ardashir I conquered the western Kushan territories in Bactria, beginning the fragmentation of the empire

sources.citation: Rosenfield, J.M. (1967). The Dynastic Arts of the Kushans. University of California Press.
Rabatak inscription of Kanishka I. c. 127 AD.
Fan Ye (范曄). Hou Hanshu (後漢書), chapter on the Western Regions. c. 445 AD.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Korean (Hangul). ETHICS-002: Joseon was a Confucian state with a rigid class system (yangban aristocracy, commoners, and slaves). The Japanese invasions of 1592-1598 (Imjin War) caused devastating destruction and mass civilian casualties. Joseon's end came through Japanese imperial pressure, leading to forced annexation in 1910.

name_variants.name: Joseon Dynasty
name_variants.source: Palais, Confucian Statecraft and Korean Institutions

name_variants.name: 朝鮮國
name_variants.context: Classical Chinese name used in official documents

name_variants.name: Chosŏn
name_variants.source: Cumings, Korea's Place in the Sun

territory_changes.description: General Yi Seong-gye overthrew the Goryeo dynasty and established Joseon, adopting Neo-Confucianism as state ideology
Japanese invasion under Toyotomi Hideyoshi (Imjin War) devastated the peninsula for seven years, destroying cultural heritage and causing massive civilian casualties through massacre and enslavement

sources.citation: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (朝鮮王朝實錄). 1392-1865. Annals of the Joseon Dynasty.
Palais, J.B. (1996). Confucian Statecraft and Korean Institutions. University of Washington Press.
Cumings, B. (2005). Korea's Place in the Sun: A Modern History. W.W. Norton.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Korean. ETHICS-002: Goguryeo is a contested entity in modern historiography -- both South Korea and North Korea claim it as Korean ancestral heritage, while Chinese historiography (Northeast Project) controversially claims it as a local Chinese state. This database records it with Korean-language primary naming per the kingdom's own cultural identity, while acknowledging the historiographic dispute.

name_variants.name: Goguryeo
name_variants.source: Byington, The History and Archaeology of the Koguryŏ Kingdom

name_variants.name: 高句麗
name_variants.context: Chinese character form used in Chinese and Japanese sources

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding by Jumong (朱蒙) at Jolbon in the Yalu River region; blends legend with early state formation
Tang-Silla alliance conquered Goguryeo after prolonged campaigns. Hundreds of thousands of Goguryeo people were forcibly relocated to Tang China

sources.citation: Kim Bu-sik (金富軾). Samguk Sagi (三國史記). 1145.
Byington, M.E. (2016). The Ancient State of Puyŏ in Northeast Asia. Harvard University Press.
Gwanggaeto Stele inscription. 414 AD.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Korean. ETHICS-002: Baekje maintained strong cultural connections with Japan and was a major conduit for transmitting Chinese culture (Buddhism, writing, art) to the Japanese archipelago. The kingdom's fall to the Tang-Silla alliance in 660 involved mass deportation of the elite and destruction of the capital.

name_variants.name: Baekje
name_variants.source: Best, A History of the Early Korean Kingdom of Paekche

name_variants.name: Paekche
name_variants.source: Best, Early Korean Kingdom of Paekche

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding by Onjo at Wiryeseong in the Han River area; historical accuracy of early dates is uncertain
Combined Tang-Silla forces conquered Baekje. The royal family and 12,000 subjects were deported to Tang China. Japanese reinforcements were defeated at the Battle of Baekgang (663)

sources.citation: Kim Bu-sik (金富軾). Samguk Sagi (三國史記). 1145.
Best, J.W. (2006). A History of the Early Korean Kingdom of Paekche. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Vietnamese (Chữ Quốc ngữ romanization); the original Chữ Hán form is 大越. ETHICS-002: Đại Việt's southern expansion (Nam tiến) over centuries systematically conquered and absorbed the Champa kingdom and parts of the Khmer Empire, displacing or assimilating Cham and Khmer populations. This is a well-documented case of Vietnamese colonial expansion at the expense of indigenous Southeast Asian peoples.

name_variants.name: Đại Việt
name_variants.source: Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư

name_variants.name: 大越
name_variants.source: Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư

name_variants.name: Annam
name_variants.context: Chinese exonym (安南) meaning 'Pacified South', considered derogatory by Vietnamese

territory_changes.description: Emperor Lý Thánh Tông renamed the kingdom Đại Việt, consolidating Lý dynasty control over the Red River delta region
Lê Thánh Tông invaded and conquered the Champa kingdom, sacking the capital Vijaya and killing or displacing over 60,000 Cham people, beginning the final absorption of Champa into Vietnam

sources.citation: Ngô Sĩ Liên. Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư (大越史記全書). c. 1479.
Taylor, K.W. (2013). A History of the Vietnamese. Cambridge University Press.
Li Tana (1998). Nguyễn Cochinchina: Southern Vietnam in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries. Cornell SEAP.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian (گورکانیان, Gurkaniyan), the court and literary language. Timur himself spoke Chagatai Turkic. ETHICS-002: Timur's conquests were among the most destructive in pre-modern history. The sack of Isfahan (1387) killed an estimated 70,000; the invasion of India (1398) resulted in the massacre of 100,000 prisoners at Delhi. Total death toll from Timurid campaigns may have reached 17 million. These atrocities must not be sanitized.

name_variants.name: Timurid Empire
name_variants.source: Manz, The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane

name_variants.name: Тимуридов
name_variants.source: Bartold, Four Studies on Central Asia

territory_changes.description: Timur seized power in Samarkand, beginning three decades of devastating military campaigns across Central Asia, Persia, India, and Anatolia
Timur invaded India, defeating the Delhi Sultanate and sacking Delhi. An estimated 100,000 prisoners were massacred before the battle. The city was devastated and depopulated.

sources.citation: Manz, B.F. (1989). The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane. Cambridge University Press.
Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi. Zafarnama. c. 1424.
Bartold, V.V. (1962). Four Studies on the History of Central Asia. Brill.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian (اشکانیان, Ashkanian), from the dynastic founder Arsaces. The Greek exonym 'Parthia' derives from the region of Parthava. ETHICS-002: The Parthian Empire was Rome's principal eastern rival; Western historiography has traditionally privileged Roman perspectives. Parthian sources are scarce, and much of what we know comes from hostile Greco-Roman or later Sasanian accounts that deliberately diminished Parthian achievements.

name_variants.name: Parthian Empire
name_variants.context: English academic convention from Greek exonym

territory_changes.description: Arsaces I led the Parni tribe in revolt against the Seleucid Empire, establishing an independent kingdom in Parthava
Mithridates I conquered Mesopotamia from the Seleucids, capturing Seleucia on the Tigris and establishing Parthian control over the Fertile Crescent

sources.citation: Strabo. Geography, Book XI. c. 7 BC.
Daryaee, T. (2009). Sasanian Persia: The Rise and Fall of an Empire. I.B. Tauris.
Brosius, M. (2006). The Persians: An Introduction. Routledge.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian (ساسانیان, Sasanian), from the dynasty's ancestor Sasan. Middle Persian self-designation was Ērānšahr ('Empire of the Aryans'). ETHICS-002: Sasanian state Zoroastrianism involved periodic persecutions of religious minorities (Christians, Manichaeans, Jews). The centuries-long Romano-Sasanian wars devastated frontier populations. The Arab-Islamic conquest of 651 ended the empire but involved both military destruction and cultural transformation.

name_variants.name: Ērānšahr
name_variants.context: Middle Persian self-designation meaning 'Empire of the Aryans'

territory_changes.description: Ardashir I defeated the last Parthian king Artabanus IV at the Battle of Hormozdgan, establishing the Sasanian dynasty

Arab-Muslim forces completed the conquest of the Sasanian Empire following the Battle of Nahavand (642). Last Sasanian king Yazdegerd III was murdered in 651, ending the empire and beginning the Islamization of Iran

sources.citation: Daryaee, T. (2009). Sasanian Persia: The Rise and Fall of an Empire. I.B. Tauris.
Kār-Nāmag ī Ardaxšēr ī Pābagān (Book of the Deeds of Ardashir). c. 6th century AD.
Christensen, A. (1944). L'Iran sous les Sassanides. Copenhagen.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian (سلجوقیان, Saljuqiyan), the administrative and literary language of the empire, though the ruling dynasty was Oghuz Turkic. ETHICS-002: The Battle of Manzikert (1071) opened Anatolia to Turkic settlement, beginning the demographic transformation that would eventually result in modern Turkey. The Seljuks' role as Sunni champions against Shia Fatimids shaped sectarian conflicts that persist today.

name_variants.name: السلاجقة
name_variants.context: Arabic form used in contemporary Islamic sources

territory_changes.description: Tughril Beg defeated the Ghaznavids at the Battle of Dandanaqan, establishing Seljuk control over eastern Iran and Central Asia
Alp Arslan defeated the Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV at the Battle of Manzikert, opening Anatolia to Turkic migration and settlement that would permanently transform the region's demographics

sources.citation: Ibn al-Athir. al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh (الكامل في التاريخ). c. 1231.
Bosworth, C.E. (1996). The New Islamic Dynasties. Edinburgh University Press.
Cahen, C. (1968). Pre-Ottoman Turkey. Sidgwick & Jackson.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in medieval Greek. ETHICS-002: Trebizond was a Pontic Greek state that survived the fall of Constantinople to the Fourth Crusade. Its conquest by the Ottoman Mehmed II in 1461 led to the gradual disappearance of the Pontic Greek community through conversion, assimilation, and later (20th century) population exchanges and genocide.

name_variants.name: Empire of Trebizond
name_variants.source: Miller, Trebizond: The Last Greek Empire

name_variants.name: Trabzon İmparatorluğu
name_variants.source: Bryer & Winfield, The Byzantine Monuments and Topography of the Pontos

territory_changes.description: Alexios I Komnenos established an independent Greek state on the Black Sea coast following the Fourth Crusade's destruction of the Byzantine Empire
Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II conquered Trebizond, ending the last Greek empire. The Komnenian dynasty was deposed, and the population was gradually Islamized and Turkified over subsequent centuries

sources.citation: Miller, W. (1926). Trebizond: The Last Greek Empire of the Byzantine Era. London.
Bryer, A. & Winfield, D. (1985). The Byzantine Monuments and Topography of the Pontos. Dumbarton Oaks.
Michael Panaretos. Chronicle of Trebizond. c. 1370s.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian, the administrative language. The Mongol rulers gradually adopted Persian language and culture. ETHICS-002: The Ilkhanate was established through Hulagu Khan's devastating westward campaigns, which included the destruction of Baghdad (1258) -- one of the most catastrophic events in Islamic civilization, with the death toll estimated at 90,000 to 1 million. The destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate and its libraries represented an irreparable cultural loss.

name_variants.name: Hülegü Khanate
name_variants.source: Boyle, Cambridge History of Iran vol. 5

territory_changes.description: Hulagu Khan's forces sacked Baghdad, destroying the Abbasid Caliphate. The Grand Library of Baghdad (Bayt al-Hikma) was destroyed, the caliph was executed, and estimates of civilian deaths range from 90,000 to 1 million
Following the death of Abu Sa'id, the Ilkhanate fragmented into rival successor states as Mongol authority disintegrated across Iran and the Middle East

sources.citation: Rashid al-Din Hamadani. Jami al-Tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles). c. 1307.
Morgan, D. (2007). The Mongols. 2nd ed. Blackwell.
Boyle, J.A. (1968). Cambridge History of Iran, vol. 5: The Saljuq and Mongol Periods.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Persian, the lingua franca of Central Asian administration. The Chagatai Turkic language also developed as a literary language within this khanate. ETHICS-002: The Chagatai Khanate's formation was part of the Mongol Empire's division, which had been built through devastating conquests. Internal conflicts between sedentary and nomadic factions shaped Central Asian history for centuries.

name_variants.name: Chagatai Khanate
name_variants.source: Biran, The Empire of the Qara Khitai

territory_changes.description: Chagatai Khan received Central Asian territories as his ulus (appanage) following the division of Genghis Khan's empire
The khanate split into the Western Chagatai (later seized by Timur) and Eastern Chagatai (Moghulistan) khanates, ending unified Chagataid rule

sources.citation: Rashid al-Din Hamadani. Jami al-Tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles). c. 1307.
Biran, M. (2009). 'The Mongol Transformation.' In Cambridge History of Inner Asia. Cambridge University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Tatar (Алтын Урда). The entity was also known by its Mongol founders as Ulus of Jochi. ETHICS-002: The Golden Horde's establishment followed the devastating Mongol invasion of Rus' (1237-1240) which destroyed major cities like Kiev, Vladimir, and Ryazan, killing hundreds of thousands. The 'Tatar Yoke' over Russian principalities lasted over two centuries. Russian nationalist historiography has sometimes exaggerated the yoke's negative effects, while Tatar historiography emphasizes the era's cultural achievements.

name_variants.name: Golden Horde
name_variants.source: Halperin, Russia and the Golden Horde

name_variants.name: Золотая Орда
name_variants.source: Halperin, Russia and the Golden Horde

territory_changes.description: Batu Khan's forces invaded and devastated the Rus' principalities, destroying major cities including Ryazan, Vladimir, and Kiev. Massive civilian casualties and enslavement resulted in demographic collapse across the region
The Great Stand on the Ugra River marked the effective end of Mongol-Tatar overlordship of Russia, as Ivan III of Moscow refused to pay tribute

sources.citation: Halperin, C.J. (1985). Russia and the Golden Horde. Indiana University Press.
Juvaini, Ata-Malik. Tarikh-i Jahan-Gusha. c. 1260.
Safargaliev, M.G. (1960). Raspad Zolotoi Ordy (Dissolution of the Golden Horde). Saransk.

capital_name: Chiang Mai (เชียงใหม่)
ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Northern Thai (Lanna script: ᩃᩣ᩠ᨶᨶᩣ). Thai script used here as most accessible representation. ETHICS-002: Lan Na ('Million Rice Fields') was a Tai kingdom distinct from Siam, with its own script, dialect, and cultural identity. The Burmese conquest (1558) and later Siamese incorporation eroded Lanna identity. Modern Thai nationalist historiography sometimes subsumes Lanna history into a unified 'Thai' narrative, obscuring its distinct cultural heritage.

name_variants.name: Lan Na Kingdom
name_variants.source: Penth, A Brief History of Lan Na

name_variants.name: ᩃᩣ᩠ᨶᨶᩣ
name_variants.source: Penth, A Brief History of Lan Na

territory_changes.description: King Mangrai conquered the Mon kingdom of Haripunchai and founded Chiang Mai (1296) as the new capital of the Lan Na kingdom
Burmese forces of King Bayinnaung conquered Lan Na, beginning over two centuries of Burmese suzerainty

sources.citation: Penth, H. (2004). A Brief History of Lan Na. Silkworm Books.
Wyatt, D.K. (2003). Thailand: A Short History. 2nd ed. Yale University Press.
Chiang Mai Chronicle (Tamnan Phuen Muang Chiang Mai). c. 15th-18th centuries.

capital_name: Luang Prabang (ຫລວງພະບາງ)
ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Lao (ລ້ານຊ້າງ, 'Million Elephants'). ETHICS-002: Lan Xang was the principal Lao state, and its fragmentation in 1707 into three kingdoms (Luang Prabang, Vientiane, Champasak) set the stage for Siamese and later French colonial domination of Laos. The kingdom's history is essential for understanding Lao national identity independent from Thai and Vietnamese narratives.

name_variants.name: Laṅ Xāṅ Hōm Khāo
name_variants.context: Full formal name 'Million Elephants and the White Parasol'
name_variants.source: Stuart-Fox, The Lao Kingdom of Lān Xāng

territory_changes.description: Fa Ngum unified Lao muang (principalities) into the kingdom of Lan Xang with Khmer support, establishing the first major Lao political entity
Succession disputes led to the breakup of Lan Xang into three rival kingdoms: Luang Prabang, Vientiane, and Champasak, ending the unified Lao state

sources.citation: Stuart-Fox, M. (1997). A History of Laos. Cambridge University Press.
Stuart-Fox, M. (1998). The Lao Kingdom of Lān Xāng: Rise and Decline. White Lotus Press.

name_original: ကုန်းဘောင်ခေတ်
ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Burmese (ကုန်းဘောင်ခေတ် = Konbaung era). ETHICS-002: The Konbaung dynasty's expansion involved the destruction of the Ayutthaya kingdom (1767) and repeated invasions of Arakan, Manipur, and Assam. The British conquest in three Anglo-Burmese Wars (1824, 1852, 1885) ended the dynasty and began colonial rule. The Konbaung period also saw the consolidation of Bamar dominance over ethnic minorities (Shan, Karen, Mon, Kachin).

name_variants.name: Third Burmese Empire
name_variants.source: Thant Myint-U, The Making of Modern Burma

name_variants.name: Yadanabon Kingdom
name_variants.context: Name from the last capital Mandalay (Yadanabon)

territory_changes.description: Konbaung forces under Hsinbyushin sacked and completely destroyed the Siamese capital of Ayutthaya, burning the city, looting treasures, and deporting tens of thousands of Siamese captives
Third Anglo-Burmese War: British forces conquered Mandalay and deposed King Thibaw, completing the British colonization of Burma. The royal family was exiled to India

sources.citation: Lieberman, V. (2003). Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800-1830. Cambridge University Press.
Thant Myint-U (2001). The Making of Modern Burma. Cambridge University Press.
Glass Palace Chronicle (Hmannan Yazawin). 1829.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Sindhi (Arabic script), the local language. The ruling Arghun and Tarkhan dynasties used Persian as court language. ETHICS-002: The Sindh Sultanate period encompasses multiple dynasties (Arghun, Tarkhan, Kalhora, Talpur). British conquest under Charles Napier (1843) was a classic case of imperial expansion; Napier's alleged quip 'Peccavi' ('I have sinned/Sindh') trivializes the conquest of a sovereign state.

name_variants.name: Sindh Sultanate
name_variants.context: English academic convention (covers Arghun through Talpur periods)

name_variants.name: Talpur Sindh
name_variants.context: Final ruling dynasty of independent Sindh
name_variants.source: Lambrick, Sir Charles Napier and Sind

territory_changes.description: Shah Beg Arghun conquered Sindh from the Samma dynasty, establishing Arghun-Tarkhan rule over the lower Indus region
British forces under General Charles Napier defeated the Talpur Mirs at the Battles of Miani and Hyderabad, annexing Sindh to British India in a controversial act of imperial aggression

sources.citation: Lambrick, H.T. (1975). Sind: A General Introduction. Sindhi Adabi Board.
Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai. Shah Jo Risalo. c. 1750.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Nepali (गोर्खा राज्य, Gorkha Kingdom). ETHICS-002: The Gorkha unification of Nepal under Prithvi Narayan Shah involved the military conquest and forcible integration of numerous smaller kingdoms (Malla kingdoms of the Kathmandu Valley, Limbu territories, etc.). The process of Nepali nation-building involved the imposition of Khas-Nepali language and Hindu caste norms on diverse ethnic and linguistic groups. Nepal was the last Hindu kingdom in the world until the declaration of a republic in 2008.

name_variants.name: Gorkha Kingdom
name_variants.context: Pre-unification period when Gorkha was one of many hill kingdoms
name_variants.source: Stiller, The Rise of the House of Gorkha

territory_changes.description: Prithvi Narayan Shah conquered the three Malla kingdoms of the Kathmandu Valley (Kathmandu, Patan, Bhaktapur), establishing Gorkha rule over Nepal's heartland
Treaty of Sugauli following the Anglo-Nepalese War forced Nepal to cede Sikkim, Kumaon, Garhwal, and parts of the Terai to the British East India Company, fixing Nepal's modern borders

sources.citation: Whelpton, J. (2005). A History of Nepal. Cambridge University Press.
Stiller, L.F. (1973). The Rise of the House of Gorkha. Ratna Pustak Bhandar.
Divya Upadesh (Prithvi Narayan Shah's collected teachings). c. 1774.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Chinese (匈奴), as no native Xiongnu writing system survives. The Xiongnu self-designation is unknown. This is a fundamental limitation: we know the Xiongnu primarily through Chinese sources written by their adversaries. ETHICS-002: Chinese sources are inherently biased against the Xiongnu, consistently portraying them as 'barbarians'. The relationship between the Xiongnu and the later European Huns remains academically disputed and should not be presented as established fact.

name_variants.name: Xiongnu
name_variants.source: Di Cosmo, Ancient China and Its Enemies

territory_changes.description: Modu Chanyu unified the steppe nomadic tribes into a powerful confederation, creating the first great nomadic empire of Inner Asia
The Xiongnu confederation split into Northern and Southern branches. The Southern Xiongnu submitted to the Han dynasty as vassals, while the Northern Xiongnu were eventually driven westward

sources.citation: Sima Qian. Shiji (史記), 'Xiongnu Liezhuan' chapter. c. 94 BC.
Di Cosmo, N. (2002). Ancient China and Its Enemies: The Rise of Nomadic Power in East Asian History. Cambridge University Press.
Barfield, T.J. (1989). The Perilous Frontier: Nomadic Empires and China. Blackwell.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The original Old Turkic name would be written in the Orkhon script (𐰚𐰇𐰛:𐱅𐰈𐰼𐰜). Modern Turkish is used here as the closest living linguistic descendant with ISO 639-1 code. ETHICS-002: The Göktürk Khaganate was the first Turkic state to use the endonym 'Türk' and created the earliest known Turkic writing system (Orkhon script). Their legacy is claimed by multiple modern Turkic nations, and the historical narrative is sometimes politicized in pan-Turkic nationalist contexts.

name_variants.name: Göktürk Khaganate
name_variants.source: Golden, Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples

territory_changes.description: Bumin Qaghan led the Turkic revolt against the Rouran Khaganate, establishing the first Turkic khaganate spanning from Manchuria to the Black Sea
Tang dynasty forces defeated and absorbed the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, subjugating the Turkic peoples for 50 years until the Second Turkic Khaganate was established in 682

sources.citation: Orkhon inscriptions (Bilge Qaghan and Kul Tigin steles). 732-735 AD.
Golden, P.B. (1992). An Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples. Harrassowitz.
Sinor, D. (1990). Cambridge History of Early Inner Asia. Cambridge University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in modern Uyghur script. The historical Uyghur Khaganate used Old Uyghur script derived from Sogdian. Note: the medieval Uyghurs and the modern Uyghur people of Xinjiang share a name and partial cultural continuity, but the connection is complex. ETHICS-002: The Uyghur Khaganate's conversion to Manichaeism is significant as the only known case of a major nomadic state adopting this religion. Modern Chinese historiography sometimes instrumentalizes the distinction between medieval and modern Uyghurs in the context of the Xinjiang conflict.

name_variants.name: Uyghur Khaganate
name_variants.source: Golden, Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples

name_variants.name: 回鶻
name_variants.context: Chinese designation (revised from earlier 回紇)

name_variants.name: Toquz Oghuz
name_variants.context: Turkic name for the Nine Tribes confederation that formed the Uyghur state

territory_changes.description: The Uyghur tribal confederation overthrew the Second Turkic Khaganate, establishing their own khaganate centered on the Orkhon River valley
Yenisei Kirghiz forces destroyed the Uyghur Khaganate, sacking Ordu-Baliq. Surviving Uyghurs fled south and west, establishing successor states in Gansu and Turfan

sources.citation: Golden, P.B. (1992). An Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples. Harrassowitz.
Karabalgasun inscription. 810s AD.
Mackerras, C. (1972). The Uighur Empire According to the T'ang Dynastic Histories. ANU Press.

capital_name: Shangjing (上京, modern Baarin Left Banner)
ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original uses the Chinese dynastic name (遼朝). The Khitan self-designation in Khitan script is also significant but less accessible. In Chinese historiography, the Liao is given a full dynastic history (Liaoshi). ETHICS-002: The Khitan Liao dynasty controlled sixteen prefectures of northern China that had been part of the Tang empire, creating a dual administration system (Chinese-style bureaucracy for sedentary populations, tribal governance for nomads). This administrative innovation has been downplayed in Sinocentric narratives.

name_variants.name: Liao Dynasty
name_variants.source: Twitchett & Tietze, Cambridge History of China vol. 6

name_variants.name: Khitan Empire
name_variants.context: Alternative name emphasizing the ruling ethnic group
name_variants.source: Wittfogel & Feng, History of Chinese Society: Liao

territory_changes.description: Abaoji unified the Khitan tribes and proclaimed himself emperor, establishing the Khitan state that would become the Liao dynasty
Jurchen Jin forces captured the last Liao emperor, destroying the dynasty. Remnant Khitan elites fled west and established the Qara Khitai (Western Liao) in Central Asia

sources.citation: Tuotuo (脫脫). Liaoshi (遼史). 1344.
Twitchett, D. & Tietze, K. (1994). 'The Liao.' Cambridge History of China, vol. 6.
Wittfogel, K.A. & Feng, Chia-sheng (1949). History of Chinese Society: Liao (907-1125). TAPS.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original uses the Chinese dynastic name (金朝, 'Gold Dynasty'). The Jurchen self-designation was Anchu (in Jurchen script). Like the Liao, the Jin receives a full dynastic history in Chinese historiography (Jinshi). ETHICS-002: The Jurchen conquest of northern China (1126-1127) involved the Jingkang Incident -- the capture and humiliation of two Song emperors and the enslavement of thousands of court members. The Jin also conducted devastating campaigns against the Mongols and other steppe peoples before being destroyed by the Mongol-Song alliance.

name_variants.name: Jin Dynasty (Jurchen)
name_variants.context: English convention; distinguished from the earlier Jin dynasty (265-420)
name_variants.source: Tao, The Jurchen in Twelfth-Century China

name_variants.name: Jurchen Jin
name_variants.context: Ethnic qualifier to distinguish from other Jin dynasties

territory_changes.description: Jurchen forces captured the Song capital Kaifeng in the Jingkang Incident, seizing two emperors and 3,000 court members as prisoners. Northern China was incorporated into the Jin dynasty
Combined Mongol and Southern Song forces conquered the last Jin stronghold at Caizhou, ending the dynasty. The last Jin emperor committed suicide

sources.citation: Tuotuo (脫脫). Jinshi (金史). 1344.
Tao, Jing-shen (1976). The Jurchen in Twelfth-Century China. University of Washington Press.
Franke, H. (1994). 'The Chin Dynasty.' Cambridge History of China, vol. 6.
